question text: Why is it important to draw a diagram or sketch before building a model boat?
a: To plan how materials fit together and minimize waste
b: To ensure the boat looks colorful
c: To increase the weight of the boat
d: To generate more friction on the paper
e: To make the boat aerodynamic for flight
f: To memorize the names of the parts

question text: In a rubber band-powered paddle boat, where is the energy stored before the boat starts moving?
b: In the potential energy of the twisted rubber band
c: In the weight of the cardboard
d: In the friction of the axle
e: In the air surrounding the boat
f: In the glue holding it together

question text: If you drop a flat sheet of plastic and a crumpled ball of the same plastic into a pool, the flat sheet sinks slower. Why?
a: The flat sheet has more mass
c: The flat sheet has a larger surface area pushing against the water
e: Gravity does not pull on flat objects
f: The crumpled ball becomes heavier underwater

question text: According to the physics of propulsion, if a paddle blade pushes water backward (to the rear of the boat), what is the immediate reaction?
c: The boat spins in a perfect circle
e: The boat moves sideways to the left

question text: If your paddle boat constantly turns to the right instead of going straight, what is the most likely engineering flaw?
c: The rubber band is too colorful
e: The paddle or propulsion system is uneven or off-center
f: The testing tub is too deep

question text: Why does a dense, heavy object (like a solid metal cube) typically sink in water?
c: It is repelled by the surface tension
e: It has too much air inside
f: Gravity pulls it down with more force than the buoyancy pushes it up

question text: Why are materials like waxed cardboard or plastic preferred over standard construction paper for the hull of a boat?
a: They resist water absorption and maintain structural integrity
b: They are much heavier and anchor the boat
d: They are rougher and increase friction

question text: When testing a boat, why is it valuable to set a specific distance goal (e.g., "move 1 foot")?
a: It ensures the boat sinks at the end
b: It provides a measurable standard to judge success or failure
c: It determines the color of the boat
d: It limits how much glue you can use
e: It makes the boat go faster automatically
f: It reduces the cost of materials

question text: If a boat is top-heavy (too much weight high up), what is likely to happen when it is placed in the water?
c: It will tip over or capsize due to instability

question text: Water resistance is described as a force that "competes against motion." What does this mean for a boat engineer?
a: You need to add more weight to the front
b: You must design the shape to cut through water efficiently
c: You should make the boat as wide as possible to hit more water
d: Water resistance makes the boat go faster
e: Gravity is not a factor in water
f: You should use rough materials to grab the water
